claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 18 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 18 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 18 and in approved education; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 18 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you a few questions.

First, do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do! I have a child - my son Alex. He's 18 and still lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to understand his current situation.

Is Alex currently in approved education or training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he is. He's still in college doing his A-levels.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful, thank you! Since Alex is 18 and in approved education (A-levels), he may still qualify.

Can I confirm - do you live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Just a few more questions to complete the assessment.

Does Alex work at all, and if so, is it for 24 hours or more per week?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't work. He's just focused on his studies at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent, thank you. One final question:

Does Alex receive any benefits in his own right, such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't receive any benefits in his own name.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good news! **You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Here's why:

Although Alex is 18 (over 16), you can still claim Child Benefit because:
- He is under 20 years old
- He is in approved education (his A-levels at college)
- He doesn't work 24 hours or more per week
- He doesn't receive benefits in his own right (like Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance)
- You live together in the UK

**Important note:** Your Child Benefit will stop if Alex:
- Finishes or leaves his approved education/training
- Starts working 24 hours or more per week
- Starts claiming certain benefits in his own right

Also, if you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit tax charge.

If you haven't already claimed, you can apply for Child Benefit. If you're already receiving it, you should continue to be eligible as long as Alex remains in approved education.

reasonings: Alex is 18 years old and in approved education (A-levels at college). According to the rules, you normally still qualify for Child Benefit if your child is under 20 and they stay in approved education or training. Alex meets this criterion as he is 18 and doing A-levels. Additionally, Alex does not work for 24 hours or more per week, and he does not receive benefits in his own right (such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit), both of which would disqualify him. The claimant lives in the UK with Alex, establishing responsibility for the child. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their son Alex. Although Alex is 18 years old (over 16), he qualifies because he is under 20 and remains in approved education (A-levels). He does not work 24+ hours per week and does not claim benefits in his own right. The claimant lives with Alex in the UK, meeting the responsibility requirement.

Alex meets this criterion as he is 18 and doing A-levels. Additionally, Alex does not work for 24 hours or more per week, and he does not receive benefits in his own right (such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit), both of which would disqualify him. The claimant lives in the UK with Alex, establishing responsibility for the child. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Although Alex is 18 years old (over 16), he qualifies because he is under 20 and remains in approved education (A-levels). He does not work 24+ hours per week and does not claim benefits in his own right. The claimant lives with Alex in the UK, meeting the responsibility requirement.
